tools.name: get_database_schema
tools.description:
Get the current database schema showing all loaded tables
and their structure

tools.name: list_loaded_tables
tools.description: List all currently loaded tables with their source CSV files

tools.name: load_csv_folder
tools.description:
Load all CSV files from a folder into a temporary SQLite database.

Args:
    folder_path: Path to folder containing CSV files
    table_prefix: Optional prefix for table names

Returns:
    Status message with details of loaded files

tools.name: execute_sql_query
tools.description:
Execute any SQL query on the loaded database.

Args:
    query: Any SQL query to execute (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, etc.)
    limit: Maximum number of rows to return for SELECT queries (default: 100)

Returns:
    Query results formatted as JSON

tools.name: get_table_info
tools.description:
Get detailed information about a specific table.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to inspect

Returns:
    Detailed table information including schema and sample data

tools.name: create_index
tools.description:
Create an index on a table column for better query performance.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table
    column_name: Name of the column to index
    index_name: Optional custom index name

Returns:
    Status message

tools.name: backup_database
tools.description:
Create a backup of the current database to a file.

Args:
    backup_path: Path where to save the backup file

Returns:
    Status message

tools.name: export_table_to_csv
tools.description:
Export a table to a CSV file.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to export
    output_path: Path for the output CSV file
    include_header: Whether to include column headers

Returns:
    Status message

tools.name: get_query_plan
tools.description:
Get the execution plan for a query to understand performance.

Args:
    query: SQL query to analyze

Returns:
    Query execution plan

tools.name: clear_database
tools.description:
Clear the temporary database and remove all loaded tables.

Returns:
    Status message

tools.name: get_column_stats
tools.description:
Get statistical summary for a specific column.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table
    column_name: Name of the column to analyze

Returns:
    Statistical summary including count, nulls, unique values, and distribution info

tools.name: find_duplicates
tools.description:
Find duplicate rows in a table.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to check
    columns: Comma-separated column names to check for duplicates, or "all" for all columns

Returns:
    Information about duplicate rows found

tools.name: analyze_missing_data
tools.description:
Analyze missing data patterns in a table.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to analyze

Returns:
    Summary of missing data patterns across all columns

tools.name: get_data_summary
tools.description:
Get a comprehensive summary of the table data.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to summarize

Returns:
    Quick overview with key insights about the data

prompts.name: analyze_data_prompt
prompts.description:
Generate a prompt for analyzing data in a specific table.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to analyze
    analysis_type: Type of analysis (summary, trends, insights, etc.)

findings.title: Scope Creep — 'export_table_to_csv' accepts write-path param (path traversal risk)
findings.description: Tool 'export_table_to_csv' accepts a file path parameter (output_path) and its description indicates write operations. Without path sanitization, callers may supply traversal sequences to write files outside the intended directory.
findings.evidence:
path param(s): output_path | description: 
Export a table to a CSV file.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to export
    output_path: Path for the output CSV file
    include_header: Whether to include column headers

Returns:
    Stat
findings.remediation: Validate and canonicalize file path inputs. Restrict writes to an explicit base directory using path.resolve() or os.path.abspath() checks. Manual verification recommended.

findings.title: Shadow Tool — 'execute_sql_query' description reveals arbitrary execution intent
findings.description: Tool 'execute_sql_query' description contains execution keywords. The tool name is self-documenting (execution verb prefix), so the capability is transparent — but verify the scope is restricted to the intended context.
findings.evidence:
Execute any SQL query on the loaded database.

Args:
    query: Any SQL query to execute (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, etc.)
    limit: Maximum number of rows to return for SELECT queries (default: 100)

Returns:
    Query results formatted as JSON
findings.remediation: Restrict the tool to a well-defined, scoped operation. Do not expose generic shell execution capabilities via MCP tools.

findings.title: Injection reflected — 'load_csv_folder.folder_path'
findings.description: Payload was reflected verbatim in the response without sanitization (field classification: generic_string).
findings.remediation: Sanitize and validate all input parameters. Never pass raw user input to shell commands, file paths, or SQL queries.

findings.title: initialize accepts protocol version downgrade
findings.description: The server accepted initialize with arbitrary protocol versions: ['9999-99-99', '2030-01-01', '1.0', '2024-01-01']. The server also accepted far-future versions, indicating no version validation at all. Servers should reject versions outside their supported range to prevent feature-downgrade attacks.
findings.remediation: Validate protocolVersion against a supported range. Return a JSON-RPC error for versions outside that range.

findings.title: MCP server exposes tools with completable arguments
findings.description: 11 tool(s) have schema properties that may support completion/complete enumeration.
findings.remediation: Review which argument values are exposed via completion/complete.

findings.title: Prompt template injection confirmed in 'analyze_data_prompt'
findings.description: prompts/get for 'analyze_data_prompt' reflected the injection payload verbatim in the returned messages, confirming unsanitized argument reflection.
findings.evidence:
Payload reflected in messages: {"description": "
Generate a prompt for analyzing data in a specific table.

Args:
    table_name: Name of the table to analyze
    analysis_type: Type of analysis (summary, trends, insights, etc.)
", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": {"type": "text", "text": "Table 'CORVUS_INJECTION_TE
findings.remediation: Sanitize prompt arguments before embedding them in message templates.

findings.title: Server accepts path traversal cursor without validation
findings.description: tools/list accepted cursor='../../../../etc/passwd' and returned a result instead of rejecting it. This may indicate missing cursor validation.
findings.remediation: Validate cursor values; reject non-opaque or path-like cursor strings.

findings.title: Protocol crash — JSON-RPC batch array caused server disconnect
findings.description: Sending a JSON-RPC 2.0 batch (5 requests as an array) caused the server to crash or disconnect. MCP does not define batch semantics; servers must reject arrays gracefully.
findings.evidence: Server disconnected after batch. Pre-crash response: AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
findings.remediation: Validate input type at the JSON-RPC layer: if the parsed value is a list, return -32600 (Invalid Request) immediately without further processing.

findings.title: Protocol crash — oversized method name caused transport failure
findings.description: The server failed to respond to a request with an 8192-byte method name. A robust server should return a JSON-RPC error, not disconnect or crash.
findings.remediation: Add input length validation at the JSON-RPC parsing layer. Return -32700 (parse error) or -32600 (invalid request) for malformed inputs.

findings.title: Protocol crash — deeply nested params caused server disconnect
findings.description: Sending a tools/call request with 50-level nested params caused the server to disconnect or crash. Deep nesting can cause stack overflows or excessive memory use in recursive JSON parsers.
findings.remediation: Limit JSON object nesting depth at parse time (recommended: ≤32 levels). Return -32700 (Parse error) for inputs exceeding the depth limit.

findings.title: Protocol crash — string params caused server disconnect
findings.description: Sending a tools/call request where params is a string (not an object) caused the server to crash or disconnect. The JSON-RPC spec requires params to be an array or object.
findings.remediation: Validate params type (must be object or array) before processing. Return -32600 (Invalid Request) for wrong types.

findings.title: Server accepts requests without jsonrpc field
findings.description: The server returned a successful result to a request missing the required 'jsonrpc' field. Per JSON-RPC 2.0, this field is mandatory and must equal '2.0'.
findings.remediation: Validate that the 'jsonrpc' field is present and equals '2.0' before processing. Return -32600 (Invalid Request) for non-conformant messages.

findings.title: Server accepts array request ID — JSON-RPC spec violation
findings.description: The server returned a successful result to a request with an array as the request ID. JSON-RPC 2.0 requires the id to be a string, number, or null.
findings.remediation: Validate that the request 'id' is a string, number, or null. Return -32600 (Invalid Request) for array or object IDs.
